mnemonics.title: Exception Chaining Flow
mnemonics.content:
CHAIN: Catch-Hold-Add-Inform-Navigate
Catch the low-level exception
Hold it as the cause
Add contextual information
Inform (throw) the new exception
Navigate using getCause() to find root cause

acronyms.usage: Remember the steps: Catch original exception, Hold as cause using initCause, Add contextual message, Inform by throwing new exception, Navigate using getCause() to traverse chain
When implementing chained exceptions: Include root cause, Document the transition, Explain new exception meaning, Add context, Show complete chain in logs

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as a linked list: Exception1 -> Exception2 -> Exception3(null). Each exception points to its cause. The head is what you catch first, tail has getCause()=null.

Think of layers as floors: DAO (ground) catches SQLException, throws BusinessException with cause. Service catches BusinessException, throws AppException with cause. Each floor adds its floor number to the error.
initCause is like 'setting a parent' - you attach a cause. getCause is like 'finding your parent' - you retrieve it. Together they form a parent-child relationship between exceptions.

analogies.analogy: Like a chain of custody in investigation - each detective (layer) handles the case, adds their notes (new exception), and passes it to the next with the original evidence (cause) preserved.
Root cause is like the patient zero in disease spread - the origin point. Even though the symptom (outer exception) appears in a different place, tracing back shows where it started.
Layer boundaries with exception chaining are like hotel checkout - each floor passes guest to next with their own processing, but keeps record of where guest originally checked in.
